title: Mindfulness-based interventions for psychiatric disorders: A systematic review and meta-analysis
headline: Mindfulness-based interventions for psychiatric disorders: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: A meta-analysis / systematic review of mindfulness / meditation examined emotional wellbeing. Effects on specific disorder subgroups showed the most consistent evidence in support of mindfulness for depression, pain conditions, smoking, and addictive disorders. Results support the notion that mindfulness-based interventions hold promise as evidence-based treatments.
keyFindings: Mindfulness conditions did not differ from minimal treatment conditions (d=0.38) and evidence-based treatments (d=0.09).
Effects on specific disorder subgroups showed the most consistent evidence in support of mindfulness for depression, pain conditions, smoking, and addictive disorders.
Results support the notion that mindfulness-based interventions hold promise as evidence-based treatments.
limitations: Pooled estimates combine heterogeneous studies; effect sizes vary by practice, population, and control condition.

The practice studied (mindfulness / meditation) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared directed-attention component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: Mindfulness-based interventions for psychiatric disorders: A systematic review and meta-analysis
aiSnippet: Meta-analysis / Systematic review, Clinical psychology review (2018). Mindfulness conditions did not differ from minimal treatment conditions (d=0.38) and evidence-based treatments (d=0.09). Effects on specific disorder subgroups showed the most consistent evidence in support of mindfulness for depression, pain conditions, smoking, and addictive disorders. Results support the notion that mindfulness-based interventions hold promise as evidence-based treatments.
whyItMatters: MoM shares directed attention (the focused-attention component) with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
